anomalies.description: Sudden 90% drop in death benefit value
anomalies.recommendation: Investigate policy adjustment or potential data entry error
Review rider charges and policy amendments
Verify payment method and contact policyholder

Development of Metric API for
FAST System

Aditya Sharma

BIRLA INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY &
SCIENCE, PILANI
(March – 2026)

Duration: 6 months
Date of Start: [Start Date]
Date of Submission: [Submission Date]
Title of project: Development of Metric API for FAST System
Name of the Student: Aditya Sharma
ID.NO.: [Your ID]
Discipline: Computer Science (BITS Pilani, K.K. Birla Goa Campus)
Name of the Project Manager: [Manager Name]
March 2026


--- Page 2 ---
Mentor: [Mentor Name], Designation: [Designation]
PS Faculty: Dr. Chetana Anoop Gavankar, Professor and Program Lead CSIS
Keywords: RESTful API, ASP.NET Core, SQL Server, Dapper ORM, Financial
Systems, C#
Project Areas: Backend Web Development, Database Management, Enterprise API
Integration
2


--- Page 3 ---
Abstract
This mid-semester report details the comprehensive design, architecture, and develop-
ment of a highly scalable Metric Application Programming Interface (API) for the FAST
(Financial Administration System Technology) platform at Zinnia. Operating within the
highly regulated life insurance technology sector, the primary objective of this project
was to architect a secure, RESTful endpoint capable of efficiently retrieving complex,
loan-specific policy values within a strictly bounded date range. These critical finan-
cial metrics serve as the foundational data required for generating automated, legally
compliant Whole Life (WL) Anniversary statements for policyholders.
Developed utilizing ASP.NET Core 9.0 and C#, the API heavily leverages the Dap-
per micro-ORM to facilitate high-performance, low-latency SQL Server database interac-
tions. To navigate the deeply normalized, legacy database structures of the FAST system,
a sophisticated database architecture was implemented involving complex Common Ta-
ble Expressions (CTEs), dynamic identifier lookups, and conditional aggregations. This
approach allowed the system to gather both real-time calculations and historical snap-
shot data regarding policy loan balances, accrued interest, principal disbursements, and
prevailing interest rates. Furthermore, the API bridges modern REST architecture with
legacy systems by successfully integrating with an external SOAP-based service (the CVQ
API) to fetch live net surrender values. This report systematically outlines the architec-
tural patterns (such as the Repository Pattern and Dependency Injection), the nuanced
database schema navigation, the robust implementation strategies, and the exhaustive
testing methodologies employed to elevate the API to production-ready enterprise stan-
dards.
1


--- Page 4 ---
Acknowledgements
I would like to utilize this opportunity to express my profound gratitude to the Practice
School Division at BITS Pilani, K.K. Birla Goa Campus, and to my dedicated team
members at Zinnia in Pune, whose unwavering support has been crucial during the first
half of this internship. I am deeply grateful for the aspiring guidance, technical men-
torship, and timely constructive feedback which have helped me fine-tune my software
engineering skills to meet the rigorous demands of the enterprise industry.
I would especially like to thank the Practice School Division for providing such a
wonderful opportunity to deep-dive into the corporate world and experience the current
trends of the tech industry firsthand. Transitioning into the Launchpad 2026 program at
Zinnia has profoundly helped me adapt to professional work ethics, improve my technical
communication skills, and enrich my overall backend development experience.
Finally, I would like to express my deep gratitude to my PS faculty, Dr. Chetana
Anoop Gavankar, for her constant guidance, academic support, and motivation through-
out the duration of this Practice School course.
2

--- Page 6 ---
1
Introduction
1.1
Organization Overview: Zinnia & The FAST System
Zinnia operates as a premier technology partner in the life insurance and annuities sector,
providing robust digital solutions designed to streamline complex policy administration.
At the core of Zinnia’s technological infrastructure lies the FAST (Financial Administra-
tion System Technology) platform. FAST is a highly scalable, comprehensive life insur-
ance policy administration system utilized by major financial institutions to manage the
entire lifecycle of complex insurance products, particularly Whole Life (WL) insurance
policies.
The system acts as the absolute source of truth for policyholders, responsible for
meticulously tracking policy origination, daily financial transactions, complex loan ac-
tivities against policy cash values, premium payment processing, and storing immutable
historical financial snapshots across a heavily normalized SQL Server database architec-
ture.
1.2
Domain Context: Whole Life Insurance Metrics
To understand the technical requirements of the API, it is essential to understand the
business domain. Whole Life insurance policies build a ”Cash Value” over time. Policy-
holders are legally permitted to borrow against this cash value (a Policy Loan). These
loans accrue interest, and policyholders can make ad-hoc payments toward the principal
or the interest. At the end of every policy year (the Anniversary), the insurance provider
is required to generate an Anniversary Statement detailing the exact financial movements
of that loan over the past 365 days.
1.3
Project Overview & Objectives
As business requirements and downstream applications evolve, the FAST platform re-
quired the development of a new, dynamic Metric API. Prior systems relied on rigid,
batch-processed data extracts. This new component is designed to determine precise
financial values dynamically, within any specific date range, utilizing the PolicyNumber,
StartDate, and EndDate as input parameters.
Scope of Work: Initially, this endpoint is tasked with calculating and retrieving
nine specific Loan-related values required to populate the Whole Life (WL) Anniver-
sary statements. A critical architectural constraint mandated by the engineering team
is extensibility. The API must be inherently dynamic and scalable, ensuring that future
4


--- Page 7 ---
developers can easily append new data fields (such as withdrawal metrics or premium
tax data) without requiring a foundational system redesign or complex database migra-
tions. The immediate scope involves designing the GET /policy/v1/metrics endpoint,
establishing a highly optimized data access layer, and ensuring secure, token-based au-
thentication.
5


--- Page 8 ---
2
Background & Literature Review
Before commencing the development of the Metric API, a thorough architectural review
was conducted to select the appropriate technologies that align with the enterprise stan-
dards of the FAST platform.
2.1
RESTful Architecture vs. Alternatives
Representational State Transfer (REST) was chosen as the architectural style for this API.
While alternatives like GraphQL offer highly flexible data querying, REST was selected
for its standard HTTP verb utilization, simplicity in caching, and seamless integration
with existing Zinnia microservices. The stateless nature of REST ensures that the Metric
API can scale horizontally across multiple servers without session management overhead.
2.2
ORM Selection: Dapper vs. Entity Framework
In the .NET ecosystem, Entity Framework (EF) Core is the dominant Object-Relational
Mapper (ORM). However, for this specific microservice, Dapper (a Micro-ORM) was
chosen.
• Performance: The Metric API requires aggregating millions of transaction rows.
EF Core’s LINQ-to-SQL translation adds significant overhead and can sometimes
generate suboptimal execution plans for complex Common Table Expressions (CTEs).
Dapper allows developers to write raw, highly tuned SQL while still providing the
convenience of mapping database results directly to C# objects.
• Codebase Consistency: The existing FAST backend utilizes raw parameterized
SQL for its heavy financial calculations; utilizing Dapper maintains paradigm con-
sistency across the codebase.
2.3
Bridging Protocols: REST to SOAP
A unique challenge in this project is the requirement to fetch the MaxLoanValueAvailable.
While historical data is in the database, this specific metric requires a live actuarial cal-
culation. The external system responsible for this, the Cash Value Quote (CVQ) engine,
operates on an older Simple Object Access Protocol (SOAP) utilizing XML over HTTP.
A key part of the background research involved understanding how to wrap a legacy
SOAP invocation within a modern RESTful JSON response without exposing the XML
complexities to the downstream consumers.
6


--- Page 9 ---
3
System Architecture
The Metric API is built on a modern, decoupled architecture designed for maintainability
and robust unit testing.
3.1
The Repository Pattern & Dependency Injection
The system rigorously adheres to the Repository Pattern. This design pattern isolates
the data access logic from the business and presentation logic.
1. The Controller Layer (MetricController.cs): Acts purely as the entry point.
It receives the HTTP request, validates the input parameters, and returns the
appropriate HTTP status codes (200 OK, 400 Bad Request). It contains absolutely
zero database logic.
2. The Interface (IMetricRepo.cs): Defines the contract.
The controller only
knows about the interface, not how the data is fetched.
3. The Data Layer (MetricRepo.cs): Implements the interface. It handles the SQL
query construction, Dapper execution, and the external SOAP API call.
Dependency Injection (DI) is utilized in Program.cs via builder.Services.AddScoped<IMetri
MetricRepo>();. This inversion of control allows the framework to instantiate the repos-
itory automatically per HTTP request, ensuring efficient resource management and al-
lowing for easy mocking during future unit testing phases.
3.2
Security Context
Because the API exposes sensitive financial and personally identifiable information (PII)
linked to a PolicyNumber, security is paramount.
The endpoint is decorated with a
TokenValidationFilterAttribute. Before the controller logic even executes, this mid-
dleware intercepts the request, decodes the provided Bearer token, validates the crypto-
graphic signature against the internal identity provider, and verifies that the consumer
has the correct scopes to access policy metrics.
7


--- Page 10 ---
4
Database Design & Relational Mapping
The most complex aspect of the Metric API lies within the FAST system’s database
schema. The database is highly normalized, requiring deep joins and an understanding
of transaction lifecycles.
4.1
Schema Navigation
The FAST system utilizes a strict naming convention: Cm Opt [code] [FullName] S.
Key tables utilized include:
• Cm Opt Poh PolicyHdr S: Validates the existence of the policy and retrieves its
primary internal identifier (Poh ID).
• Cm Opt Txh TrxHdr S: The Transaction Header table. This stores the metadata for
every financial event, including the EFFECTIVEDATE and the TRXSTATUS.
• Cm Opt Tre TrxRes S: The Transaction Results table.
A massive table contain-
ing the calculated financial aftermath of a transaction (e.g., Tre LOANBALANCE,
Tre TrxLoanPrincipalPaid).
• Cm Opt Thp TrxHdrPaymt S & Cm Opt Tdb TrxHdrDisburse S: Payment and Dis-
bursement tables used to track physical money movement.
4.2
The Setup Query: Dynamic Identifiers
A major pitfall in legacy development is ”magic strings” or hardcoded GUIDs. During
development, it was discovered that the GUID representing a ”Completed Transaction”
or a ”Loan Payment” might differ between the Development (DEV), Quality Assurance
(QA), and Production (PROD) database environments.
To mitigate this, the API executes a preliminary ”Setup Query”. This query dynam-
ically joins the Cm Sys Txs TrxStatus I and Cm Sys Txt TrxType I reference tables to
fetch the exact GUIDs for the current environment. This ensures 100% portability of the
compiled code across all server environments.
4.3
The Main CTE (Common Table Expression)
To avoid making nine separate round trips to the database for the nine required metrics,
a unified SQL query utilizing a Common Table Expression (CTE) was engineered.
8


--- Page 11 ---
The CTE acts as a temporary result set. It allows the API to break down the com-
plex logic into manageable, isolated subqueries that the SQL Server query optimizer can
process in parallel.
For example, calculating the YTDAccruedNewLoanAmount requires isolating all
transactions flagged as ”Loans” within the StartDate and EndDate. However, the FAST
system might store the monetary value in the Disbursement table (Tdb) OR the Payment
table (Thp) depending on how the money was routed. The SQL query utilizes a LEFT
JOIN to both tables and a CASE WHEN statement to dynamically select the non-null value,
summing them together accurately.
4.4
Concurrency and NOLOCK
Given that FAST is a highly active OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) system, run-
ning heavy aggregate queries can lock tables, preventing other systems from inserting new
transactions. To prevent this, every table in the FROM and JOIN clauses utilizes the WITH
(NOLOCK) hint. This tells SQL Server to read uncommitted data, bypassing read locks.
While this introduces a minimal risk of ”dirty reads”, it is an accepted enterprise stan-
dard for analytical/reporting endpoints to ensure the core system’s throughput remains
unhindered.
9


--- Page 12 ---
5
External API Integration: The CVQ Adapter
While historical data (like past loan payments) can be aggregated from the database,
the MaxLoanValueAvailable is a dynamic, live value. It relies on real-time actuarial
math based on the current net surrender value of the policy.
This data is owned by a separate microservice: the Cash Value Quote (CVQ) system.
1. Protocol Transformation: The API leverages an existing PolicyHdrAdapter
class. The C# backend constructs an XML payload containing the PolicyNumber,
CompanyId, and the statement EndDate.
2. Invocation: This payload is sent over HTTP to the internal SOAP endpoint.
3. Parsing: The resulting XML response is deserialized, and the QuoteResult node
is traversed to extract the numerical net surrender value.
4. Resilience: Network calls to external services are the most common point of failure
in microservices. This call is wrapped in a strict try/catch block. If the CVQ
system times out, the API catches the exception, logs the error asynchronously
to the monitoring dashboard, and safely defaults the MaxLoanValueAvailable to
0.00. This ensures that a failure in one external dependency does not crash the
entire Metric API response.
10


--- Page 13 ---
6
Testing & Quality Assurance
Quality assurance was integrated continuously throughout the development lifecycle rather
than as a final step.
6.1
Input Validation Testing
The MetricController acts as a strict gatekeeper. Robust testing was conducted via
Postman to ensure malformed requests were rejected gracefully.
• Culture-Invariant Parsing: Dates in HTTP requests can be ambiguous (e.g.,
03/04/2026 could be March 4th or April 3rd depending on server locale). The con-
troller utilizes DateTime.TryParseExact with CultureInfo.InvariantCulture
enforcing strict yyyy-MM-dd formatting. Tests were run passing invalid formats,
ensuring the API returned a dynamic 400 Bad Request detailing exactly which
parameter failed.
• Whitespace Trimming: Tests were conducted injecting trailing spaces into the
PolicyNumber. The API was programmed to sanitize these inputs before database
execution to prevent zero-result returns.
6.2
Data Verification Methodology
Ensuring the mathematical accuracy of the API was paramount.
1. A sample of 15 diverse Whole Life policies was identified using the FAST UI (in-
cluding policies with no loans, multiple loans, and paid-off loans).
2. The API was executed via Postman for these policies.
3. Concurrently, raw, line-by-line verification queries were executed directly in SQL
Server Management Studio (SSMS) to manually calculate the sums and balances.
4. The FAST front-end UI was consulted to view the visual transaction history.
5. All three sources (API JSON, SSMS output, and UI displays) were cross-referenced
to ensure 100% parity.
11


--- Page 14 ---
7
References
1. Microsoft Documentation. ASP.NET Core Web API documentation. Retrieved
from https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/aspnet/core/web-api
2. Dapper Tutorial. Dapper Micro ORM for ADO.NET. Retrieved from https://
dapper-tutorial.net/
3. FAST System Internal Confluence & Architecture Documentation (Zinnia Internal
Resources).
4. Whole Life (WL) Statement Mapping Internal Specification Document (Ticket:
LAAS-30574).
5. Fowler, M. (2002).
Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture.
Addison-
Wesley Professional. (Reference for Repository Pattern implementation).
12
